I see that ONAP community is starting to design VNF template, I also suggest we have a Kubernetes node like VDU. In this way, containerized VNF can work well with NS.

This is my opinion about a definition of a containerized VNF. In Kubernetes, Pod is a smallest object, of course, we can use Pod as a VNF. But in this case, we have to do all things. Kubernetes provides Deployment and StatefulSets to maintain the number of Pods. Other thing is Service, it acts as a Load Balancer to Pods and some other things like ConfigMap, Horizontal Pod Autoscaler and Persistent volume. IMO, a containerized VNF should be defined by some Kubernetes objects above and expose VNF to outside by Service (Loadblancer type).

Example: because a Pod doesn't have a real IP, we can expose a containerized DNS VNF to the world by IP: 1.2.3.4 using Service.

https://msb.onap.org:80/api/multicloud-kubernetes/v0/clusters/<cloud-id>/proxy/api/v1/namespaces/{namespace}/service/<service id>

In this example, we can abstract a containerized VNF by Serivce id. Deployment, Service, Horizontal Pod Autoscaler, etc. can have the same Serivce id (name). We can manage all these object by this name.

Line 134-135 indicates that "k8s Plugin will distinguish package types based on its suffix and interact with k8s cluster appropriately".

So if the suffix is .csar, it is a CSAR. If the suffix is .tgz, it is a Helm Charts package.

Copy my comments for Patch Set 12:

Line 134-135 (PS#12) or Line 166-167 (this PS#13) indicates that "k8s Plugin will distinguish package types based on its suffix and interact with k8s cluster appropriately".

So if the suffix is .csar, it is a CSAR. If the suffix is .tgz, it is a Helm Charts package.

Thus the original design of "/<cloud-id>/package" is more scalable.

Filename in "Content-Disposition" header seems a practical way. Thank you for documenting this detail in PS#13.

"Content-Type" header may work for tgz "application/x-compressed", but not for csar because you can only use general "application/octet-stream", which is not scalable for other new types if they also use the general "application/octet-stream".

Some more details regarding uploading file, including file name, with HTTP Post method + Content-Disposition header in multipart. It is quite a standard way in HTTP client:

- RFC 1867:
  * https://tools.ietf.org/html/rfc1867
- Detailed explanation from Mozilla Developer Docs:
  * https://developer.mozilla.org/en-US/docs/Web/HTTP/Headers/Content-Disposition
- An example in Stackoverflow to upload a file:
  * https://stackoverflow.com/questions/8659808/how-does-http-file-upload-work

In essence, the action at HTTP client side to post a file is the same as to upload a file at client side (i.e. SO side from MultiVIM perspective). It is the standard way to implement it. No matter how package file changes, the way of posting it to MultiVIM is the same in SO. The client side code in SO stays the same. The API in MultiVIM stays the same.

On the other hand, if we provide separate API for different package, there is extra code in SO to differentiate file types. More importantly, when we need to add new package support in the future:
- MultiVIM needs to add new API. So not only the new code to handle new API, but also the overhead of documenting and maintaining extra APIs, and deprecating old APIs.
- SO needs to add new code to differentiate new package and post to new API.

https://wiki.onap.org/display/DW/RESTful+API+Design+Specification
why? this is not test case itself.

If I read the guideline correctly, the relevant paragraph is under section "URI Construction":

"A REST API should not include artificial file extensions in URIs to indicate the format of a message’s entity body. Instead, they should rely on the media type, as communicated through the Content-Type header, to determine how to process the body’s content. "

Here, it doesn't suggest to use file extensions in *URIs*.

Based on the context and example in the whole section of URI Construction, it really means the file extensions shouldn't be in URI. For example:
- we should use (in given example): /api/petstore/v1/pets/dogs
- we shouldn't use: /api/petstore/v1/pets/dogs.json

In our case, the URI is "<cloud-id>/package".

Both "Content-Disposition" and "Content-Type" are headers. It is not part of URI.

For double safety to distinguish the type, I agree that both should be used. In case the proprietary content type "onap-..." is not supported, we always can distinguish them.
